The **bishop of Bath and Wells** is the diocesean bishop of the [diocese of Bath and Wells](/wiki/Diocese_of_Bath_and_Wells "Diocese of Bath and Wells") in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). The bishop's seat, or [cathedra](/wiki/Cathedra "Cathedra"), is at the [Cathedral Church of Saint Andrew](/wiki/Wells_Cathedral "Wells Cathedral") in the city of [Wells](/wiki/Wells,_Somerset "Wells, Somerset"). The diocese covers most of Somerset and a small part of [Dorset](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset").

From 1090 until the [Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") the [Abbey Church of St Peter and Paul](/wiki/Bath_Abbey "Bath Abbey") in the city of [Bath](/wiki/Bath,_Somerset "Bath, Somerset") was also a cathedral of the diocese, and the double name has been retained. The bishop has the right, together with the [bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), to escort the sovereign at the [coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_British_monarch "Coronation of the British monarch"). The bishop's residence is the [Bishop's Palace, Wells](/wiki/Bishop%27s_Palace,_Wells "Bishop's Palace, Wells").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop_of_Bath_and_Wells&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Diocese of Bath and Wells](/wiki/Diocese_of_Bath_and_Wells "Diocese of Bath and Wells")

Somerset originally came under the authority of the [Bishop of Sherborne](/wiki/Bishop_of_Sherborne "Bishop of Sherborne"), but [Wells](/wiki/Wells,_Somerset "Wells, Somerset") became the seat of its own **bishop of Wells** from 909. King [William Rufus](/wiki/William_Rufus "William Rufus") granted Bath to a royal physician, [John of Tours](/wiki/John_of_Tours "John of Tours"), Bishop of Wells and Abbot of Bath, who was permitted to move his episcopal seat for [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset") from Wells to [Bath](/wiki/Bath,_Somerset "Bath, Somerset") in 1090, thereby becoming the first **bishop of Bath**. He planned and began a much larger church as [his cathedral](/wiki/Bath_Abbey "Bath Abbey"), to which was attached a priory, with the bishop's palace beside it.

In 1197 Bishop [Savaric FitzGeldewin](/wiki/Savaric_FitzGeldewin "Savaric FitzGeldewin") officially moved his seat to [Glastonbury Abbey](/wiki/Glastonbury_Abbey "Glastonbury Abbey") with the approval of [Pope Celestine III](/wiki/Pope_Celestine_III "Pope Celestine III"). However, the monks there would not accept their new **bishop of Glastonbury** and the title of **bishop of Bath and Glastonbury** was used until the Glastonbury claim was abandoned in 1219. His successor, [Jocelin of Wells](/wiki/Jocelin_of_Wells "Jocelin of Wells"), then returned to Bath, again under the title, **bishop of Bath**. The official episcopal title became **bishop of Bath and Wells** under a Papal ruling of 3 January 1245.

By the 15th century Bath Abbey was badly dilapidated. [Oliver King](/wiki/Oliver_King "Oliver King"), bishop of Bath and Wells, decided in 1500 to rebuild it on a smaller scale. The new abbey-church was completed just a few years before Bath Priory was dissolved in 1539. Then [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") considered this new church redundant, and it was offered to the people of Bath to form their parish church; but they did not buy it, and it was stripped of its glass and lead.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The last bishop in communion with Rome was deprived in 1559 but the succession of bishops has continued to the present day.

In late 2013 the [Church Commissioners](/wiki/Church_Commissioners "Church Commissioners") announced that they were purchasing the [Old Rectory](/wiki/The_Old_Rectory,_Croscombe "The Old Rectory, Croscombe"), a Grade II-listed building in [Croscombe](/wiki/Croscombe "Croscombe") for the bishop's residence.[[3]](#cite_note-900k-3) However this decision was widely opposed,[[4]](#cite_note-petition-4) including by the diocese,[[5]](#cite_note-split-5)[[6]](#cite_note-notsupport-6) and in May 2014 was overturned by a committee of the [Archbishops' Council](/wiki/Archbishops%27_Council "Archbishops' Council").[[7]](#cite_note-overrule-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The diocese and the episcopate are today part of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism").

## Assistant bishops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop_of_Bath_and_Wells&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Assistant bishops")]

Among those who have served as assistant bishops of the diocese are:

* 4 October 1852 – 10 May 1853 (res.): [George Spencer](/wiki/George_Spencer_(bishop) "George Spencer (bishop)"), commissary during Bagot's illness, and former [Bishop in Madras](/wiki/Bishop_in_Madras "Bishop in Madras")[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* late 1860s (Eden's illness): [James Chapman](/wiki/James_Chapman_(bishop) "James Chapman (bishop)"), Coadjutor-Bishop, Rector of [Wootton Courtenay](/wiki/Wootton_Courtenay "Wootton Courtenay"), a Prebendary of Wells and former [Bishop of Colombo](/wiki/Bishop_of_Colombo "Bishop of Colombo")[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* 1891–1900:[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Charles Bromby](/wiki/Charles_Bromby "Charles Bromby"), former [Bishop of Tasmania](/wiki/Anglican_Bishop_of_Tasmania "Anglican Bishop of Tasmania") (lived at [Clifton](/wiki/Church_of_All_Saints,_Clifton "Church of All Saints, Clifton") with [his son](/wiki/Henry_Bromby "Henry Bromby"))
* 1931 – 1942 (d.): [Charles de Salis](/wiki/Charles_de_Salis "Charles de Salis"), [Archdeacon of Taunton](/wiki/Archdeacon_of_Taunton "Archdeacon of Taunton") until 1938, and former [Bishop of Taunton](/wiki/Bishop_of_Taunton "Bishop of Taunton")[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* 1940 – 1943 (res.): [Edmund Sara](/wiki/Edmund_Sara "Edmund Sara"), former [Coadjutor](/wiki/Coadjutor "Coadjutor") [Bishop of Jamaica](/wiki/Bishop_of_Jamaica "Bishop of Jamaica") and later [Assistant Bishop of Hereford](/wiki/Assistant_Bishop_of_Hereford "Assistant Bishop of Hereford")[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* 1950–1967 (ret.): [Fabian Jackson](/wiki/Fabian_Jackson "Fabian Jackson"), Rector of [Batcombe](/wiki/Batcombe,_Somerset "Batcombe, Somerset") and former [Bishop of Trinidad](/wiki/Bishop_of_Trinidad "Bishop of Trinidad")[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* 1956 – 1973 (ret.): [Douglas Wilson](/wiki/Douglas_Wilson_(bishop) "Douglas Wilson (bishop)"), Canon Treasurer of Wells and former [Bishop of Trinidad](/wiki/Bishop_of_Trinidad "Bishop of Trinidad")[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop_of_Bath_and_Wells&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop_of_Bath_and_Wells&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Television")]

*[Monty Python](/wiki/Monty_Python "Monty Python")* features two skits in which the Bishop of this title is mentioned.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The 1980s [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") [historical](/wiki/Historical_fiction "Historical fiction") [sitcom](/wiki/Sitcom "Sitcom") *[Blackadder](/wiki/Blackadder "Blackadder")* features an episode entitled [Money](/wiki/Money_(Blackadder) "Money (Blackadder)") in which Hollywood actor [Ronald Lacey](/wiki/Ronald_Lacey "Ronald Lacey") plays an obese, blasphemous, self-confessed pervert who describes themselves as "the baby-eating Bishop of Bath and Wells!"[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop_of_Bath_and_Wells&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Radio")]

[*Absolute Power*](/wiki/Absolute_Power_(comedy) "Absolute Power (comedy)"), BBC radio comedy features such a Bishop.

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop_of_Bath_and_Wells&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Literature")]

[Neil Gaiman](/wiki/Neil_Gaiman "Neil Gaiman")'s 2008 work *[The Graveyard Book](/wiki/The_Graveyard_Book "The Graveyard Book")* features a character named the Bishop of Bath and Wells – he is one of a trio of ghouls who spirit the main character away.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Ralph of Shrewsbury, Bishop of Bath and Wells, appears as a character in the 1994 fantasy novel *The Dragon, The Earl and The Troll*, by Gordon Dickson.
